utilities.note: electric + water + internet; utilities notably higher than Buenos Aires

groceries.note: home cooking; food prices 30–40% higher than Buenos Aires

diningOut.note: strong chivito and parrilla culture; restaurants mid-range by LATAM standards

transit.note: bus network extensive; taxis/rideshare also available

misc.note: gym, toiletries, entertainment, buffer; stable political environment

accomSeasonality._doc: 12 monthly multipliers for RENT ONLY (Jan..Dec). 1.0 = base. Southern hemisphere; slight summer peak (Dec-Feb) for coastal districts.

evidence.quote: Remote workers can comfortably cover living expenses on a monthly budget of around USD $1,500–$2,000. A comfortable lifestyle typically requires USD $1,500 to $2,000 per month
monthly rent for a one-bedroom apartment in central neighborhoods typically ranges from USD $700 to $1,000, with higher prices in coastal areas such as Pocitos, Punta Carretas, and Carrasco
Montevideo's cost of living is estimated to be 30 to 40 percent higher than cities such as Buenos Aires or Asunción, largely due to higher prices for food, utilities, and services

evidence.claim: internet avg 150 Mbps; health insurance $80–$150/month optional
evidence.quote: Average internet speed in Montevideo is 150 Mbps, which is excellent for remote work. Basic health insurance plans range from around $80–150 per month
